frameworks.name: saliency map
frameworks.one_line: topographic map of feature contrast that captures attention bottom-up

frameworks.name: iso-feature suppression via lateral inhibition
frameworks.one_line: V1 superficial-layer mechanism proposed to create the saliency map

frameworks.name: feedforward hierarchy V1 to parietal cortex
frameworks.one_line: layer-resolved effective connectivity tests directionality of saliency transmission

core_question: Where in the cortex is the bottom-up visual saliency map generated, and how is it propagated to attentional control regions?
key_assumptions: orientation contrast between foreground and background drives saliency
cortical-layer CBV fMRI at 7T can dissociate feedforward and feedback signals
saliency map is implemented in a specific cortical layer rather than distributed equally
manipulations: orientation contrast of foreground bar against uniform background
cortical depth (superficial / middle / deep layers) as readout
brain region (V1 vs intraparietal sulcus, IPS)
key_findings: human detection of salient bars improved with larger orientation contrast
saliency-sensitive responses peaked in V1 superficial layers and IPS middle layers
layer-dependent effective connectivity indicated feedforward transmission from V1 to IPS
behavioral sensitivity correlated with V1 superficial-layer response
interpretations: V1 superficial layers build the saliency map via iso-feature lateral inhibition
this V1 map is then fed forward to parietal cortex to guide attention and eye movements
